agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature where customers can view their courier's real-time location during delivery. Two internal stakeholder teams have submitted directly contradictory requirements: Customer Success demands full GPS transparency, while Operations demands no location sharing to protect driver retention.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Courier Visibility","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"The driver/delivery person fulfilling the last-mile delivery to the customer."},{"term":"Real-time location","definition":"The GPS-derived position of the courier, updated at frequent intervals during an active delivery."},{"term":"Delivery window","definition":"The time period during which a courier is actively en route to the customer's delivery address."},{"term":"Location granularity","definition":"The level of precision at which a courier's position is exposed — ranging from exact GPS coordinates to zone-level approximation."},{"term":"Active delivery","definition":"The state of a delivery from the moment a courier departs for the customer until the parcel is handed off or marked delivered."},{"term":"Courier privacy","definition":"The operational and legal principle that a courier's precise movements should not be exposed in ways that create surveillance or safety risks."}]},"business_rules":[],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Courier is stationary for an extended period (e.g., traffic, break) and customer sees no movement","expected_behavior":"Behavior undefined until contradiction is resolved — but any solution must avoid exposing the courier to customer harassment about perceived delays."},{"scenario":"Courier deviates from expected route (multi-drop, personal stop)","expected_behavior":"Behavior undefined — exact GPS would reveal this, approximate zones would not. This is a key privacy concern driving Operations' objection."},{"scenario":"Customer uses location data to intercept or confront courier","expected_behavior":"A known safety risk in the courier industry. Any solution must account for courier physical safety."},{"scenario":"Delivery marked 'arriving' but courier is not nearby","expected_behavior":"Depends on granularity model chosen. Exact GPS exposes the lie; zone-based may mask it. Both have business implications."},{"scenario":"Regulatory jurisdictions with strict worker-location-sharing laws (e.g., EU GDPR, state-level privacy laws)","expected_behavior":"Legal review required — sharing exact worker GPS with third parties may violate labor privacy regulations in certain jurisdictions."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High risk in both directions. Losing driver retention (Operations' concern) directly increases recruitment and onboarding costs — industry average $3,000-$5,000 per driver. Losing customer satisfaction (Customer Success' concern) increases churn and reduces LTV. Neither team's position can be dismissed on financial grounds alone.","operational":"Driver retention is an existential operational concern in last-mile logistics. A shortage of couriers directly degrades delivery capacity, SLA compliance, and service availability. Simultaneously, lack of tracking visibility increases WISMO (Where Is My Order) support volume, which is a major cost center.","user":"Customers want delivery predictability — they want to know WHEN their package arrives so they can plan. Operations' insight suggests that exact GPS is not what customers actually need; they need confidence and predictability. However, Customer Success may be conflating 'transparency' with 'exact coordinates' without evidence that customers specifically demand GPS-level precision.","risk":"CRITICAL — shipping either requirement as stated creates serious business harm. Exact GPS risks driver attrition and potential legal liability. No visibility risks customer dissatisfaction and increased support costs. Additionally, labor relations and potential regulatory exposure (GDPR, worker surveillance laws) are unaddressed.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"CRITICAL — Exact GPS sharing creates surveillance experience, safety risks, and is actively causing attrition. Their voice is underrepresented in this requirements conflict."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"HIGH — Want delivery predictability and transparency. Actual need may be satisfiable without exact GPS (e.g., ETA, zone-level tracking, delivery stages)."},{"group":"Customer Success team","impact":"HIGH — KPIs likely tied to CSAT and NPS. Need a tracking solution that demonstrably improves customer confidence."},{"group":"Operations team","impact":"HIGH — KPIs likely tied to driver retention, fleet capacity, delivery SLA. Cannot absorb further driver attrition."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"MEDIUM-HIGH — Not yet consulted. Sharing worker GPS with customers has regulatory implications in multiple jurisdictions."},{"group":"Support/WISMO team","impact":"MEDIUM — Better tracking reduces inbound volume. But the form of tracking matters less than its effectiveness at setting expectations."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"Confidence is very low because the spec contains a direct, unresolved contradiction between two authoritative stakeholder teams, both presenting their position as non-negotiable. No acceptance criteria can be defined until this is resolved. Additionally, Legal/Compliance has not weighed in, and the actual customer need (exact GPS vs. predictability) has not been validated with user research. The spec as written is unshippable.","high_confidence_areas":["This is a genuine business contradiction that cannot be resolved by engineering alone","Courier privacy and safety is a real domain constraint in logistics","Customer desire for delivery predictability is well-established in the industry","Both teams have legitimate business concerns grounded in real operational data"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether customers actually need exact GPS or would be equally satisfied with zone-level / ETA-based tracking","The legal implications of sharing courier GPS in all operating jurisdictions","Whether a compromise position (e.g., approximate location, delivery stages) would satisfy Customer Success KPIs","The actual driver attrition numbers attributable specifically to location sharing vs. other factors"],"assumptions":["Both teams are acting in good faith based on real signals from their respective constituencies","No executive-level decision has already been made that overrides one team","Legal/Compliance review has not yet been conducted on courier location sharing"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires exact GPS visibility for customers ('at all times during delivery'). Operations requires zero location visibility for customers ('courier location must never be shown'). These are mutually exclusive requirements from equally authoritative internal stakeholders. Neither team has presented this as negotiable. This cannot be resolved at the engineering level — it requires a business-level decision with executive sponsorship.","affected_stakeholders":["Couriers/Drivers","Customers","Customer Success","Operations","Legal/Compliance"],"options":["A: Side with Customer Success — show exact GPS. Accept driver attrition risk and potential legal exposure.","B: Side with Operations — show no location. Accept increased WISMO support volume and potential customer churn.","C: Facilitate a compromise — show approximate/zone-level location or delivery stage indicators (e.g., 'Courier is nearby', ETA countdown) that gives customers predictability without surveilling drivers. This requires BOTH teams to move off their stated positions.","D: Run user research first — validate whether customers actually need exact GPS or whether ETA/stage-based tracking satisfies their real need. Use data to break the deadlock."],"recommendation":"Option D first, then Option C. The underlying customer need is almost certainly 'delivery predictability,' not 'surveillance capability.' User research will likely show that ETA-based or zone-level tracking satisfies customers while protecting courier privacy. Industry leaders (e.g., DoorDash, Uber Eats) use approximate/smoothed location for exactly this reason — it balances transparency with worker dignity. However, this recommendation requires executive sponsorship to override both teams' stated non-negotiable positions. Legal must also review any location-sharing approach before implementation."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Cannot define acceptance criteria for a feature with mutually exclusive requirements from two authoritative stakeholder teams.","business_rule_broken":"No coherent business rule can be extracted — the two stated rules ('always show exact GPS' vs. 'never show location') are logical contradictions."},{"violation":"Courier safety and privacy implications have not been evaluated by Legal/Compliance.","business_rule_broken":"Any feature exposing worker location to third parties must pass legal review — this is a hard prerequisite in logistics platforms operating in GDPR or worker-protection jurisdictions."}]}
